questions.question:
Let <M> be the encoding of a Turing machine as a string over {0, 1}. Let L = {<M> | M is a Turing machine that accepts a string of length 2014}. Then L is:

A. decidable and recursively enumerable
B. undecidable but recursively enumerable
C. undecidable and not recursively enumerable
D. decidable but not recursively enumerable
questions.to_find: Whether L is decidable, undecidable, recursive, or recursively enumerable

understand.plain: We have a language L that contains encodings of Turing machines that accept at least one string of length 2014. We need to determine if we can always decide membership in L. This is a non-trivial property of Turing machines, so by Rice's theorem it is undecidable. However, we can semi-decide it: run all TMs on all strings of length 2014 in a dovetailed manner and accept if any is accepted.

keywords.explain: A language for which there exists a Turing machine that always halts and correctly accepts or rejects every input.
keywords.example: The set of all even numbers is decidable.

keywords.explain: A language for which there exists a Turing machine that accepts all strings in the language (but may loop on strings not in the language).
keywords.example: The halting problem is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: Any non-trivial semantic property of Turing machines is undecidable. A property is non-trivial if some TMs have it and some don't.
keywords.example: Whether a TM accepts the empty language is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: A Turing machine M is given as input. We ask: does M accept at least one string of length 2014? This is a non-trivial property, so it is undecidable by Rice's theorem. But we can enumerate all TMs and all strings of length 2014 in parallel, accepting if we find a match.

given.aim: Classify L = {<M> | M accepts some string of length 2014} among decidable, undecidable, RE, and non-RE.

terms.term: L = {<M> | M accepts some string of length 2014}
terms.meaning: L contains encodings of TMs that have at least one string of length 2014 in their accepted language.
terms.example: If M accepts 'aa' (length 2), then <M> would be in L for the length-2 version.
terms.connects: This is the language whose decidability we must determine.

terms.meaning: A property P of TMs where some TMs satisfy P and some don't, meaning P is about the language L(M).
terms.example: Accepting at least one string of length 2014 is non-trivial: some TMs do, some don't.
terms.connects: By Rice's theorem, any such property is undecidable.

terms.meaning: A language is RE if we can enumerate (accept) all members, even if we cannot always reject non-members.
terms.example: We can run M on all strings of length 2014 in parallel and accept <M> if M ever accepts any of them.
terms.connects: This shows L is at least RE.

given.plan: Apply Rice's theorem to show L is undecidable (it's a non-trivial semantic property of TMs). Then show L is RE by describing a semi-decider: dovetail the execution of M on all strings of length 2014; if any is accepted, accept <M>.

steps.title: Apply Rice's Theorem to show undecidability
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem: any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a TM is undecidable.
steps.apply: The property P = 'M accepts at least one string of length 2014' is a semantic (language) property. It is non-trivial because: (1) a TM that accepts everything satisfies P; (2) a TM that accepts nothing does not satisfy P. By Rice's theorem, deciding P is undecidable. So L is not recursive (not decidable).
steps.note: Rice's theorem applies here because we're asking about L(M), not about the TM's structure.

steps.title: Show L is Recursively Enumerable
steps.formula_raw: A language is RE if there exists a TM that accepts all its members (and may diverge on non-members).
steps.apply: There are finitely many strings of length 2014 (2^2014 strings over {0,1}, or a finite set over any finite alphabet). Construct a semi-decider: on input <M>, use dovetailing to simulate M on each of these finitely many strings. If M accepts any string of length 2014 at any point in the simulation, accept. Since the set of strings of length 2014 is finite, this is equivalent to running M on each string and checking if it halts and accepts. If M accepts any such string, we eventually find out. So L is RE.
steps.note: L is not co-RE because we cannot confirm that M accepts NO string of length 2014 (M could run forever on some of them).

solution.result: L is undecidable but recursively enumerable. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Rice's Theorem for undecidability; semi-decidability argument for RE membership using finite string enumeration.

questions.question:
For any two languages L1 and L2 such that L1 is context-free and L2 is recursively enumerable but not recursive, which of the following is/are necessarily true?

I. L1' (Complement of L1) is recursive
II. L2' (Complement of L2) is recursive
III. L1 intersection L2 is context-free
IV. L1 union L2 is recursively enumerable

A. I only
B. III only
C. III and IV only
D. I and IV only
questions.to_find: Which of the four statements about L1 and L2 are necessarily true given the stated conditions

understand.plain: L1 is a context-free language (CFL) and L2 is recursively enumerable (RE) but not recursive. We need to check which of four statements always hold. CFLs are a subset of recursive languages, and recursive languages are closed under complement. RE languages are closed under union. The intersection of a CFL and an RE language is not necessarily CFL.

keywords.explain: A language recognized by a pushdown automaton or generated by a context-free grammar. All CFLs are recursive.
keywords.example: The language {a^n b^n | n >= 0} is a CFL.

keywords.explain: A language accepted by a TM that may loop forever on non-members; there is no TM that decides the language.
keywords.example: The halting problem is RE but not recursive.

keywords.explain: The class of RE languages is closed under union and intersection. The class of recursive languages is closed under complement.
keywords.example: If A and B are RE, then A union B is RE.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="420" height="200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x="5" y="5" width="410" height="190" rx="10" class="bg"/><ellipse cx="210" cy="100" rx="195" ry="85" fill="none" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2"/><text x="210" y="40" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">RE languages</text><ellipse cx="210" cy="105" rx="145" ry="60" fill="none" stroke="#1D9E75" stroke-width="2"/><text x="210" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Recursive</text><ellipse cx="210" cy="115" rx="90" ry="38" fill="none" stroke="#D85A30" stroke-width="2"/><text x="210" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">CFL</text><text x="210" y="125" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">L1 (CFL)</text><text x="340" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub no">L2 (RE, not Rec)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Hierarchy diagram showing RE languages contain Recursive languages, which contain CFLs. L1 is in the CFL region, L2 is in RE but outside the Recursive region.

given.aim: Identify which of the four statements necessarily hold given L1 is CFL and L2 is RE but not recursive.

terms.meaning: L1 belongs to the class of CFLs, which are strictly contained within recursive languages.
terms.example: Since CFL subset recursive, L1 is also recursive.
terms.connects: Being recursive means L1' (complement) is also recursive by closure.

terms.term: L2 is RE but not recursive
terms.meaning: L2 is accepted by some TM but no TM decides it. Its complement L2' is not RE.
terms.example: Halting problem H is RE; H' is not RE.
terms.connects: L2' is not even RE, so statement II is false.

terms.meaning: L1 union L2 contains strings in L1 or L2 (or both).
terms.example: Since L1 is recursive (hence RE) and L2 is RE, their union is RE.
terms.connects: RE is closed under union, making statement IV true.

given.plan: Check each statement: (I) L1 is CFL => L1 is recursive => L1' is recursive (TRUE). (II) L2 is RE not recursive => L2' is not RE, hence not recursive (FALSE). (III) Intersection of CFL and RE is not necessarily CFL (FALSE). (IV) Union of two RE languages is RE (TRUE).

steps.title: Evaluate statement I: L1' is recursive
steps.formula_raw: CFL subset Recursive; Recursive closed under complement.
steps.apply: L1 is context-free => L1 is recursive (every CFL is decidable). The class of recursive languages is closed under complement. Therefore L1' is recursive. Statement I is TRUE.
steps.note: Every CFL is decidable: we can use the CYK algorithm or run a pushdown automaton that always halts.

steps.title: Evaluate statement II: L2' is recursive
steps.formula_raw: If L is RE but not recursive, then L' is not RE (hence not recursive).
steps.apply: L2 is RE but not recursive. If L2' were recursive, it would also be RE. Then both L2 and L2' would be RE, which would make L2 recursive — a contradiction. So L2' is not recursive. Statement II is FALSE.
steps.note: A language is recursive iff both it and its complement are RE.

steps.title: Evaluate statement III: L1 intersection L2 is context-free
steps.formula_raw: CFLs are not closed under intersection with RE languages.
steps.apply: CFL intersection RE is not necessarily CFL. For example, take L1 = {a^n b^n c^m | n,m >= 0} (CFL) and L2 = some RE language; the intersection need not be CFL. In general, CFL intersected with RE gives at most an RE language (since RE is closed under intersection). But we can find a counterexample where the result is recursive but not CFL. Statement III is NOT necessarily true.
steps.note: CFLs ARE closed under intersection with regular languages (by the Pumping Lemma and product construction), but not with arbitrary RE languages.

steps.title: Evaluate statement IV: L1 union L2 is recursively enumerable
steps.formula_raw: RE is closed under union: if A and B are RE, then A union B is RE.
steps.apply: L1 is CFL => L1 is recursive => L1 is RE. L2 is RE. The union of two RE languages is RE (run both acceptors in parallel; accept if either accepts). Therefore L1 union L2 is RE. Statement IV is TRUE.
steps.note: The union may not be recursive since L2 is not recursive — it is RE but may not be decidable.

solution.result: Statements I and IV are necessarily true. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: Closure properties of CFL, recursive, and RE language classes; the complement characterization of recursive languages (L is recursive iff both L and L' are RE).

questions.answer: C. L1, L2 are recursive and L3 is not recursive
questions.to_find: Which of L1, L2, L3 are recursive (decidable)

understand.plain: We have three languages about Turing machines. L1 asks if M takes at least 2016 steps on some input. L2 asks if M takes at least 2016 steps on all inputs. L3 asks if M accepts the empty string epsilon. For L1 and L2, running 2016 steps is a bounded computation — we can always simulate for exactly 2016 steps. L3 is about acceptance, which involves undecidable halting.

keywords.explain: A language L is decidable if there exists a TM that halts on all inputs and correctly decides membership.
keywords.example: {<M> | M halts within 100 steps on input w} is decidable.

keywords.explain: Simulating a TM for at most k steps always terminates since at most k transitions are made.
keywords.example: Simulate M on input w for up to 2016 steps — this always halts, so it is decidable.

keywords.explain: Any non-trivial semantic (language) property of TMs is undecidable.
keywords.example: 'M accepts epsilon' is a non-trivial property, so L3 is undecidable.

understand.visual_alt: Three Turing machine properties: L1 (exists input with >= 2016 steps), L2 (all inputs take >= 2016 steps), L3 (accepts epsilon). L1 and L2 involve bounded simulation and are decidable; L3 is a semantic property and is undecidable.

given.aim: Determine which of L1, L2, L3 are recursive languages.

terms.term: L1 = {<M> | M takes at least 2016 steps on some input}
terms.meaning: There exists some input on which M executes at least 2016 transitions.
terms.example: If M loops forever on any input, it certainly takes >= 2016 steps on that input.
terms.connects: We can check this by simulating M on all inputs up to some length for exactly 2016 steps (bounded), making it decidable.

terms.term: L2 = {<M> | M takes at least 2016 steps on all inputs}
terms.meaning: For every possible input, M runs for at least 2016 steps.
terms.example: A TM that always runs in a 2016-step loop before halting or accepting satisfies this.
terms.connects: We can check this by simulating M on all inputs up to the point where it would halt in fewer than 2016 steps — bounded simulation works.

terms.meaning: The Turing machine M accepts the empty string.
terms.example: This is a non-trivial semantic property of M's language.
terms.connects: By Rice's theorem, this is undecidable.

given.plan: For L1: use bounded simulation — on input <M>, enumerate all strings and simulate M on each for 2016 steps; if any simulation reaches 2016 steps, accept. This is decidable. For L2: similarly, check if every short run of M on every input takes >= 2016 steps. For L3: apply Rice's theorem — accepting epsilon is a non-trivial property, so L3 is undecidable.

steps.title: Decide L1: M takes >= 2016 steps on some input
steps.apply: To decide if <M> is in L1: enumerate all strings w_1, w_2, ... in some order. For each w_i, simulate M on w_i for exactly 2016 steps. If M has not halted within 2016 steps (i.e., it took at least 2016 steps), accept. We need to check up to strings of length at most the number of states * tape alphabet size (by pigeonhole, if M takes >= 2016 steps on some input, we can find one by bounded search). Actually, L1 can be decided: if M's transition function takes >= 2016 steps on any string, this can be discovered by a finite bounded search. L1 is recursive.
steps.note: The key insight: 'takes at least k steps' is a syntactic/bounded property checkable by finite simulation.

steps.title: Decide L2: M takes >= 2016 steps on all inputs
steps.formula_raw: L2' = {<M> | M takes fewer than 2016 steps on some input} is decidable.
steps.apply: L2's complement L2' = {<M> | there exists some input w such that M halts in < 2016 steps on w}. We can decide L2': enumerate all inputs up to length 2016 (finite set), simulate M on each for 2016 steps; if M halts in < 2016 steps on any of them, accept L2'. This is a bounded simulation and always halts. Since L2' is decidable, L2 is also decidable (recursive languages are closed under complement). L2 is recursive.
steps.note: Deciding L2 is equivalent to deciding L2', which is a bounded computation problem.

steps.title: Show L3 is undecidable
steps.formula_raw: Rice's Theorem: any non-trivial property of L(M) is undecidable.
steps.apply: L3 = {<M> | M accepts epsilon} = {<M> | epsilon in L(M)}. The property 'epsilon in L(M)' is: (a) a property of L(M) (semantic), and (b) non-trivial — the TM that accepts everything has epsilon in its language, while the TM that accepts nothing does not. By Rice's theorem, L3 is undecidable (not recursive).
steps.note: L3 is in fact RE (we can semi-decide it: run M on epsilon and accept if M accepts), but it is not recursive.

solution.result: L1 and L2 are recursive; L3 is not recursive. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Bounded simulation argument for decidability of step-count properties; Rice's theorem for undecidability of semantic Turing machine properties.

questions.answer: C. L = {<M> | M is a PDA such that L(M) = {0}^*}
questions.to_find: Which of the given languages about automata encodings is NOT recursive

understand.plain: We are given languages that consist of encodings of automata (DFAs or PDAs) with specific language properties. For DFAs, we can algorithmically check whether L(DFA) equals a given regular language by minimizing both and checking isomorphism — this is decidable. For PDAs, checking if L(PDA) equals a specific context-free language is undecidable in general (CFL equivalence is undecidable).

keywords.explain: Given two DFAs, deciding if they accept the same language is decidable (minimize both and check structural isomorphism, or check if their symmetric difference is empty).
keywords.example: Check if L(DFA1) = {0}^*: minimize DFA for {0}^*, minimize DFA1, compare.

keywords.explain: Deciding if two PDAs (or a PDA and a CFG) accept the same language is undecidable. CFL equivalence is undecidable.
keywords.example: Is L(PDA) = {0}^* is undecidable — this is a non-trivial semantic property of PDAs.

keywords.explain: A language is recursive if there is a TM that decides it (halts and accepts/rejects every input).
keywords.example: All DFA-based language properties like emptiness, membership, equivalence are decidable.

understand.visual_alt: DFA properties (emptiness, equivalence, membership) are decidable. PDA/CFG equivalence is undecidable. The question asks which encoding language is not recursive, pointing to PDAs.

given.aim: Identify which language about automaton encodings is not recursive (undecidable).

terms.term: L = {<M> | M is a DFA with L(M) = {0}^*}
terms.meaning: The set of DFA encodings whose language is exactly {0}^*.
terms.example: The DFA with states q0 (start, accept on '0' to q1), q1 (accept), dead state otherwise, accepts {0}^* = {epsilon, 0, 00, ...}.
terms.connects: DFA equivalence is decidable: construct the symmetric difference and check if it is empty. So this language is recursive.

terms.term: L = {<M> | M is a PDA with L(M) = {0}^*}
terms.meaning: The set of PDA encodings whose language is exactly {0}^*.
terms.example: Checking if a PDA accepts exactly {0}^* requires solving CFL equivalence, which is undecidable.
terms.connects: CFL equivalence being undecidable means this language is not recursive.

terms.meaning: Given two PDAs (or CFGs), it is undecidable whether they generate the same language.
terms.example: This was proved by undecidability of Post Correspondence Problem reduction.

given.plan: Check each option: DFA problems (emptiness, equivalence to a fixed regular language) are all decidable since we can minimize DFAs and compare. PDA equivalence is undecidable because CFL equivalence is undecidable. Identify the PDA option.

steps.title: Analyze DFA-based languages (options A and B)
steps.formula_raw: DFA language equivalence is decidable in polynomial time.
steps.apply: Options A and B both involve DFAs. Checking if L(DFA) = {0}^* (option A) or L(DFA) = {0} (option B) reduces to DFA equivalence with a fixed DFA. Algorithm: construct DFA M1 for the target language, construct symmetric difference M1 XOR M, check if symmetric difference is empty (using reachability). This is decidable. Both A and B are recursive languages.
steps.note: All decision problems for DFAs (emptiness, universality, equivalence, membership) are decidable.

steps.title: Analyze PDA-based language (option C)
steps.formula_raw: CFL equivalence: given two CFGs G1 and G2, is L(G1) = L(G2)? This is undecidable.
steps.apply: Option C asks: is L(PDA) = {0}^*? This is a CFL equivalence problem — does the PDA generate the same language as the regular language {0}^*? Even though {0}^* is regular, deciding CFL = regular language is undecidable in general (since it would let us solve CFL universality, which is undecidable). Formally, since CFL universality (is L(PDA) = Sigma^*?) is undecidable, checking L(PDA) = {0}^* is similarly undecidable. Option C is NOT recursive.
steps.note: CFL universality (checking if a PDA accepts all strings) is undecidable, and checking equality with any specific CFL (including a regular language) is also undecidable.

solution.result: The language in option C — {<M> | M is a PDA such that L(M) = {0}^*} — is NOT recursive. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Decidability of DFA language equivalence (polynomial-time); undecidability of CFL/PDA language equivalence (CFL universality is undecidable).
